blocks.content: There is a video on Kit Jenkins' phone, unlabeled, filed between footage from the festival and a grocery list voice memo he never deleted.

It begins with the field. The April dark, the sound of the town somewhere behind it, the particular silence of a piece of land at the edge of night. Then the fireflies — a few, blinking in and out, too early in the season to be sure of themselves.

Then it turns. A woman's face in low light. Dark hair. Eyes that have been crying and aren't quite done and don't seem to care. She is looking at something just above the lens — at him, past the glass — and the look on her face is the particular look of someone who has just given a person the most important news of their life and is watching it land.
He says: you look — I want to remember how you look right now.
She says: I've been crying for four days.
He says: I know. That's part of it.
She looks at the lens. She is still. She lets herself be seen.
She says: what are you going to do with all this footage.
He says: give it to our kid. One day.

And in the four minutes and twelve seconds of that video — in the field, in the dark, with the early fireflies and the town behind them and everything that came before them pressing in from both sides — you can see exactly what twenty years of knowing someone looks like when it finally comes home to rest.
